coredata.dc:title: One-pot green synthesis of N,S co-doped biomass carbon dots from natural grapefruit juice for selective sensing of Cr(VI)
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Shipeng
Huo, Xinzhu
Zhao, Haoran
Dong, Yahui
Cheng, Qian
Li, Yu
coredata.dc:description: The synthesis of biomass carbon dots (BCDs) using natural products has attracted increasing attention in the past few years owing to its advantages in terms of abundant sources and green environmental protection. However, the low luminescence quantum yield (QY) of BCDs is still a bottleneck for practical applications. Herein, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped biomass carbon dots (N,S-BCDs) with high QY (84.93%) were successfully prepared by one-pot green hydrothermal method using natural grapefruit juice and urea as carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively. The as-obtained N,S-BCDs showed an average size of 3.8 nm with strong blue luminescence and excitation-dependence fluorescence behavior. Furthermore, good selectivity and sensitivity were noticed toward hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) due to the dynamic quenching of fluorescence. The limit of detection (LOD) was determined as 0.155 μM, and the linearity was extended from 0 to 400 μM. Furthermore, the paper sensors for the visual semi-quantitative rapid detection of Cr(VI) were constructed by using the as-obtained N,S-BCDs, which reveals the great potential applications as fluorescent probes for the detection of heavy metal ions in the environment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N,S-BCDs
Green synthesis
Fluorescence
Paper sensors
Hexavalent chromium

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Environmental water samples with quantitative Cr(VI) detection
Selectivity and sensitivity toward detection of Cr(VI)
Quantitative detection of Cr(VI) in environmental water samples

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2022 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and (b) XRD pattern of N,S-BCDs (the inset of (a) shows the particle size distribution histogram).
(a) FTIR and (b) XPS spectrum of N,S-BCDs. The high-resolution (c) C1s XPS, (d) N1s XPS, (e) O1s XPS and (f) S2p XPS of spectra of N,S-BCDs, respectively.

(a) Absorption, excitation and emission spectra of N,S-BCDs. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra with different excitation wavelengths. (c) Fluorescence emission spectra with different concentration of N,S-BCDs. (d) Effect of radiation time with UV light at 430 nm on the luminescence intensity of N,S-BCDs. (e) The luminescence intensity of N,S-BCDs with different pH. (f) Effect of NaCl concentration on fluorescence intensity of N,S-BCDs.

(a) Relative fluorescence intensity of the N,S-CDs aqueous solution with different ions excited at 430 nm. C M n+=1 mM. (b) Influence on the fluorescent signal of the presence of foreign ions with coexisting Cr(VI). (c) PL spectra of N,S-BCDs with different Cr(VI) (0∼400 μM) concentrations excitated at 430 nm. (d) Dependence of F0-F/F0 on the Cr(VI) concentration (0∼400 μM); (Inset shows a linear relationship between Cr(VI) concentration and fluorescence intensity (0∼100 μM).
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of N,S-BCDs, Cr(VI) and N,S-BCDs+Cr(VI) system. (b) Lifetime decay curves of N,S-BCDs and N,S-BCDs+Cr(VI).
Paper-based sensing obtained from N,S-BCDs for the detection of different concentration of Cr(VI).
Schematic illustration for preparation of N,S-BCDs and detection of Cr(VI).
Detection of Cr(VI) in different water samples (n = 5).
Contrast of different BCDs for sensing of Cr(VI)

head.ce:title: One-pot green synthesis of N,S co-doped biomass carbon dots from natural grapefruit juice for selective sensing of Cr(VI)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science & Technology (Ministry of Education), College of Material Science and Engineering, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science & Technology (Ministry of Education)
College of Material Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aKey Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science & Technology (Ministry of Education), College of Material Science and Engineering, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, P R China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bCollege of Science, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The synthesis of biomass carbon dots (BCDs) using natural products has attracted increasing attention in the past few years owing to its advantages in terms of abundant sources and green environmental protection. However, the low luminescence quantum yield (QY) of BCDs is still a bottleneck for practical applications. Herein, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped biomass carbon dots (N,S-BCDs) with high QY (84.93%) were successfully prepared by one-pot green hydrothermal method using natural grapefruit juice and urea as carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively. The as-obtained N,S-BCDs showed an average size of 3.8 nm with strong blue luminescence and excitation-dependence fluorescence behavior. Furthermore, good selectivity and sensitivity were noticed toward hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) due to the dynamic quenching of fluorescence. The limit of detection (LOD) was determined as 0.155 μM, and the linearity was extended from 0 to 400 μM. Furthermore, the paper sensors for the visual semi-quantitative rapid detection of Cr(VI) were constructed by using the as-obtained N,S-BCDs, which reveals the great potential applications as fluorescent probes for the detection of heavy metal ions in the environment.

ce:keyword.ce:text: nitrogen and sulfur co-doped biomass carbon dots

ce:section.ce:para: The continuous development of modern industry has caused great damage to the environment, including the serious impact of heavy metals. The presence of heavy metal ions in the environment can enter living organisms through skin contact, food intake, and breathing to affect the health of both humans and animals [1]. Chromium with various oxidation states ranging from Cr(II) to Cr(VI) is a typical heavy metal pollutant present in drinking water, which can be released through metallurgy, electroplating, pigments, and leather tanning. Among chromium ions, Cr(VI) is considered to be the most toxic, mutagenic, and carcinogenic chemical state [2]. Cr(VI) may interact with the body's proteins and enzymes to result in their inactivation or even harm. In addition, Cr(VI) is non-biodegradable, thereby accumulating in the body in great quantities, leading to acute injury [3]. Consequently, the development of new methods for the rapid and quantitative detection of Cr(VI) is very important.

Current methods for the detection of Cr(VI) consist of spectrophotometry [4], electrochemical methods [5] like adsorption stripping voltammetry [6], atomic absorption spectrometry [7], and liquid chromatography [8]. However, such methods are complicated and require specific laboratory conditions coupled with high-cost instruments and equipment, thereby not conducive to rapid on-site detection. These features seriously hinder their practical applications in various fields.

By contrast, fluorescence analysis based on fluorescence quenching is easy to operate coupled with strong selectivity, high sensitivity, and broad application prospects. Thus, this route attracts great attention for determining inorganic ions, organic molecules, and biomolecules [9,10]. Meanwhile, fluorescence materials CDs have become of interest in sensing due to their unique fluorescence characteristics, such as resistance to photobleaching, high photoluminescence, good light stability, and green/facile synthesis [11–14]. Besides, the abundant surface functional groups combined with the good biocompatibility and low toxicity have made CDs potential in bioimaging and sensing [15,16]. Recently, CDs-based fluorescence detection has made a lot of progress in Cr(VI) detection owing to its rapid, simple, and accurate advantages. For example, Sakaew et al. synthesized CDs with QY of 32.34% via hydrothermal method using onion extract as a carbon resource, and confirmed the LOD of 11.7 μM toward the detection of Cr(VI) [17]. Wang et al. prepared two kinds of CDs from wool and pig hair, respectively, and proved the detecting Cr(VI) with LODs of 16.8 nM and 29.6 nM, respectively [18]. Similarly, Li et al. reported that CDs from apple peel with a LOD of 0.73 μM for the detection of Cr(VI) [19]. However, despite the determination of Cr(VI) by CDs, it is still a challenge for a fast and visual method for detection of Cr(VI) owing to the disadvantages of low QY, poor selectivity, and the preparation method of introducing secondary contamination.

In the present work, we proposed a low cost, ecofriendly method for preparing N,S-BCDs with high QY using natural grapefruit juice and urea as carbon and nitrogen sources. The obtained N,S-BCDs with strong blue luminescence shows higher QY (84.93%), better optical stability and strong resistant to photo bleaching. Subsequently, the selective detection and anti-interference experiments to heavy metal were investigated using the gained N,S-BCDs, which indicates that they have highly sensitivity and selectivity for detecting Cr(VI). And the quenching mechanism of N,S-BCDs for Cr(VI) was elucidated. Furthermore, the paper sensors were constructed using the as-obtained N,S-BCDs to realize the visual semi-quantitative rapid detection of Cr(VI), suggesting potential applications in the environmental field.

The grapefruits were obtained from a local supermarket (Harbin, China) and served as the raw material. Urea, Nitric acid, CoCl2, MgCl2, MnCl2, CuCl2, FeCl3, FeCl2, CaCl2, AgNO3, K2Cr2O7, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, Cd(NO3)2·4H2O, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, Ba(NO3), Pb(NO3)2, Al(NO3)3·9H2O, Cr(NO3)3·9H2O, NaCl, NaBr, KCl, NaClO2, Na2SiO3·9H2O, Na2SO3, Na2CO3 were purchased from XinTian Chemical Reagents Ltd. (Harbin, China). The deionized water used here was self-made in our lab through a Kertone-mini water purification system (Clever-Q30 UT, Zhiang instrument (Shanghai) Co., Ltd) all over the experiments. All of used reagents were analytical grade and without any further purification.

N,S-BCDs were synthesized via hydrothermal method according to our previous research [20], the prepared process was followed as Scheme 1. In detail, firstly, the grapefruit were peeled and the sarcocarp were cut into small pieces, the mixture was obtained by squeezing the small pieces, then centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min, and followed by filtration of the supernatant to get pure natural grapefruit juice. Secondly, 20 mL pulp free grapefruit juice were mixed with 20 mL aqueous solution of urea (1 mol/L) and stirred for 15 min. Then the mixture was added into a 100 mL tetrafluoroethylene reactor and heated in an air furnace at 180°C for 6 hours. After natural cooling, large particles and impurities were removed by centrifugation. The supernatant was poured into dialysis bag (MwCO=3500D) for dialyzing 48 h. Finally, the products were concentrated at 80°C and freeze drying.

Quinine sulfate (QY=54% in 0.1M H2SO4 at 360 nm) was used as the standard sample to determine the QY of N,S-BCDs by means of comparison. The concentration of quinine sulfate was controlled at a particular value, which make the absorbance intensity be consistent with that of N, S-BCDs. The QY of N,S-BCDs can be calculated by the following equation:
Q
Y 1
%=
Q
Y 0
%
A 0
A 1
I 1
I 0
[21]. Where QY, A and I represent quantum yield, the absorbance at the excitation wavelength of the sample, and the integrated area of luminescence intensity correspondingly. In addition, the subscript “1” and“0” represent the N,S-BCDs and the standard quinine sulfate, respectively. And the detailed test data as shown in Table S1.

To evaluate the selectivity of detection, Cr6+, Fe2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Ba2+, Ni+, Ag+, Zn2+, Al3+, Cr3+, K+, SiO3 2−, SO3 2−, ClO−, Br−, CO3 2− ions were investigated using the obtained N,S-BCDs. In brief, 1mL of phosphate buffered solution (pH=6) was added to 2 mL of N,S-BCDs solution (0.5 g/L), and the fluorescence intensity of the prepared sample was F0 at 430 nm excitation wavelength. Similarly, 0.33 mL of different ionic solutions with a concentration of 10 mM were added to the 3 mL N,S-BCDs solution diluted by phosphate, and the concentration of the added ionic solution was precisely controlled to be 1 mM. At the excitation wavelength of 430 nm, the fluorescence intensity of the prepared sample was F. The sensitivity of Cr(VI) ions and anti-interference was explored by a series of Cr(VI) ions with different concentration (0∼400 μM) or a series of Cr(VI) and foreign ions with fixed concentration, and the detailed procedure was consistent with the selectivity experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Environmental water samples with quantitative Cr(VI) detection
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the sensing capability of N,S-BCDs in environmental water samples, the river water and tap water was collected from the Majiagou river (Harbin, China) and our laboratory. And the luminescence intensities were measured at 400 nm excitation wavelength. The tap water samples were boiled for 5 minutes to remove the residual chlorine. Then, all the water samples were centrifuged at 10 000 RPM for 10 min and pass through 0.22 μm membrane filter to eliminate the insoluble components. After that, different concentrations of Cr(VI) standard solutions were added into the above water samples to calculate the recovery rate of Cr(VI) in the real water samples. A relative standard deviation (RSD) was determined after five acquisition of data under similar experimental conditions.

TEM images were acquired using a Tecnai G2 F20 electron microscope (FEI, Holland) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. XRD pattern of N,S-BCDs were determined using a DMax/-RB2500V X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku Co., Japan) with a Cu-Kα radiation (λ=1.54056 Å). FT-IR was performed using a Perkin Elmer TV1900 instrument (Waltham, MA, USA). Fluorescence spectra were measured by Fluoromax-4 fluorescence spectrometer. UV-vis absorption spectroscopy was obtained using a TU-1901 spectrophotometer (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co.Ltd.China). XPS of N,S-BCDs were performed using K-Alpha with Al X-rays at 140W (THERMO, USA).

In this report, N,S-BCDs were synthesized by a facile green hydrothermal method using grapefruit juice as a carbon and sulfur resource, while urea was used as a nitrogen dopant to increase the luminescence intensity of N,S-BCDs. The morphology and size of each obtained sample were investigated by TEM [20]. As shown in Fig. 1 (a), N,S-BCDs with an average size of about 3.8 nm have spherical shape and good dispersion. The XRD peak at around 2Ɵ=23.3° corresponded to the (002) graphitic-like carbon structure, revealing amorphous structure [22].

The surface functional groups were further studied by analyzing the chemical structure and elemental composition of each as-prepared sample. The FT-IR and XPS data are gathered in Fig. 2 . Representative peaks of N,S-BCDs are observed in the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2(a)). The absorption peaks at 3666 cm−1 and 3184 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibrations of –OH and N-H, corresponding to the presence of hydroxyl and –NH2 groups on N,S-BCDs surfaces [23]. The existence of small peaks at 2966 cm−1 and 2908 cm−1 represents the stretching vibrations of sp2 and sp3 in C-H groups, while the peaks at 2343 cm−1, 1922 cm−1, and 1657 cm−1 are related to C-N/S-H, CN bond, and stretching vibration of C=O bond, respectively [24]. In addition, the C=C stretching vibration is recorded at 1588 cm−1 [25] and the N-H bending vibration is noticed at 1378 cm−1, while the 1052 cm−1 peak can be ascribed to –SO3-/C-O-C groups [26]. The peak at 698 cm−1 is characteristic of C-S bond. Thus, the surface of synthesized N,S-BCDs is rich in oxygen-containing groups, such as carboxyl, carbonyl, and hydroxyl, leading to N,S-BCDs with excellent hydrophilicity.

The full-scan survey for N,S-BCDs is shown in Fig. 2(b). Four main peaks are recorded at 168 eV, 284 eV, 399 eV, and 531 eV, representing S2p, C1s, N1s, and O1s XPS characteristic peaks, respectively. The corresponding atomic concentrations are determined as 4.75%, 58.91%, 9.99%, and 26.36%, respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of each element is shown in Fig. 2(c)∼(f). A total of five independent separated peaks are extracted from C1s (Fig. 2(c)). These are located at 283.8 eV, 284.5 eV, 285.4 eV, 286.4 eV, and 288.2 eV, and can be related to C-C, C=C, C-N, C=N and C=O species, respectively. The N1s spectrum of the N,S-BCDs (Fig. 2(d)) confirms the presence of amino N (398.9 eV), pyrrolic-N (399.9 eV), and N-H (401.3 eV) bond. In Fig. 2(e), three peaks can be extracted from the O1s XPS spectrum, assigned to C=O (530.7 eV), C-OH (531.6 eV), and S-O (532.9 eV). Furthermore, the S2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 2(f)) of N,S-BCDs exhibits characteristic peaks of -C-SO2- (167.5 eV), -C-SO3- (168.3 eV), and -C-SO4- (169.4 eV) bond [27]. Therefore, C=C, C=O, -NH2, -OH and C-S chemical groups are present on N,S-BCDs surfaces. These multiple functional groups do not only make the N,S-BCDs highly hydrophilic, but also will broaden their applications in metal ions sensing and biomedical fields.

The fluorescence luminescence characteristics of the materials were analyzed by UV-vis and fluorescence spectrometry. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), a strong absorption peak are noticed around 271 nm with some weak absorption peaks at 300∼350 nm caused by the π-π* electron transition of the C=C bond and n-π* transition of the C=O bond (black line) [27,28]. In addition, it can be seen that the optimal excitation and the emission wavelength of N,S-BCDs is located at 430 nm, 512 nm respectively. Fig. 3(b) shows that N,S-BCDs have obvious fluorescence dependence under the excitation wavelengths from 300 nm to 480 nm. The highest fluorescence intensity is observed at 430 nm excitation wavelength. In addition, the intensity of the emission fluorescence excited at 430 nm is the same as that of the emission fluorescence excited at 420 nm. Therefore, 430 nm is selected as the optimal excitation wavelength. Furthermore, the QY of N,S-BCDs (Ex: 430 nm) is measured as (84.93%) using quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 (QY=0.54%) as a reference. Fig. 3(c) reveals that the intensity of fluorescence increases with the decrease in the concentration of N,S-BCDs due to the fluorescence quenching effect [29]. The highest fluorescence intensity is obtained at the concentration of 0.5g/L (Fig. 3(c)), which is consistent with what we see with the naked eye (Fig.S1).

The optical stability of prepared N,S-BCDs is the key factors for its application, herein, the effect of exposure time, pH and ionic strength on their luminescence performance was investigated. Fig. 3(d) indicates that the fluorescence intensity remains unchanged even if irradiated under a UV light at 430 nm for 60 min, indicating that the N,S-BCDs is highly resistant to photo bleaching. Fig. 3(e) presents the high luminescence intensity and almost constant in pH range 4-11. In addition, the maximum intensity is observed at pH=6, which is chosen as the best condition of sensing platform for heavy ions. Furthermore, the influence of ionic strength was also explored on the fluorescence stability of the N,S-BCDs in the presence of different concentration of NaCl solution. Interestingly, there is no significant change in the luminescence behavior even at high concentration, suggesting the surface functional groups of the N,S-BCDs are nonionizable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity toward detection of Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of N,S-BCDs toward metal ions was evaluated by determining the fluorescence quenching performance in the presence of different ions (Cr6+, Fe2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Ba2+, Ni+, Ag+, Zn2+, Al3+, Cr3+, K+, SiO3 2−, SO3 2−, ClO−, Br−, CO3 2−) with 0.5g/L N,S-BCDs aqueous solution at 430 nm excitation wavelength. Compared to other metal ions (Fig. 4 (a)), a significant decline of N,S-BCDs fluorescence intensity is obtained in the presence of Cr(VI) owing to the high fluorescence quenching effect on Cr(VI). Subsequently, the anti-interference experiments of N,S-BCDs for Cr(VI) detection were investigated with the above ions (anions and cations), as shown in Fig. 4(b). It is found that only Cr(VI) has the ability to quench the luminescence intensity of N,S-BCDs, while other ions has a little or no quenching effects, which indicates that N,S-BCDs had an excellent selectivity and sensitivity for Cr(VI) detection.

The detection range and limit of detection (LOD) were determined by recording the variation in fluorescence intensity of N,S-BCDs as a function of concentration of Cr(VI). As shown in Fig. 4(c), the fluorescence intensity of N,S-BCDs decreases gradually as Cr(VI) concentration in aqueous solution (0∼400 μM). The relationship between F0-F/F0 value and concentration of Cr(VI) is shown in Fig. 4(d). Here, F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of Cr(VI). A good linear relationship is noticed at lower concentrations of (0∼100 μM) with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.99173. The linear equation can be expressed as: F0-F/F0=0.00433x+0.2059, where x represents Cr(VI) concentration. According to the equation LOD=
3 σ
S
, the LOD of N,S-BCDs solution can be calculated as 0.155 μM. Note that σ is the generalized 3-fold standard deviation of fluorescence intensity of 11 blank samples of N,S-BCDs solution, and S is the slope of the linear relationship between F0-F/F0 and Cr(VI) concentration. Hence, the data proves the strong sensitivity of N,S-BCDs toward Cr(VI) combined with lower LOD. The tentative guideline value for total human chromium intake proposed by the World Health Organization (WHO) was determined as 0.05 mg/L (0.96 μM) [30]. Therefore, the sensitivity of N,S-BCDs toward Cr(VI) completely meet the requirements of WHO for Cr(VI) detection in liquid samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative detection of Cr(VI) in environmental water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The tap water and river water was selected to unveil the practical application of Cr(VI) detection in environmental water samples. The above water samples were spiked with different concentrations of Cr(VI) ions (20 μM, 30 μM and 60 μM), and an overview of the respective retrievals could be found in Table 1 . The fluorescence responses of N,S-BCDs to Cr(VI) concentrations were 19.44, 28.78 and 57.28 μM in tap water and 19.21, 28.35 and 56.25 μM in river water, respectively. Simultaneously, the spiked recoveries of Cr(VI) based on N,S-BCDs were calculated to be 93.74∼97.23% with RSD<2.92%, which indicated that N,S-BCDs based sensing platform was highly accurate and practical for the detection of Cr(VI) ions in environmental water samples.

To figure out PL quenching mechanism of N,S-BCDs for Cr(VI), the UV-vis absorption and fluorescence lifetime were explored. The UV-vis absorption spectra of N,S-BCDs, Cr(VI) and N, S-BCDs + Cr(VI) are shown in Fig. 5 (a). It can be seen that there are two characteristic absorption peaks at 260 nm and 360 nm in the UV-vis absorption spectra of Cr(VI) (blue line). Interesting, the UV-vis spectra of N,S-BCDs + Cr(VI) are basically consistent with the UV-vis spectrum of N,S-BCDs and Cr(VI), which indicates that the quenching mechanism of N,S-BCDs for Cr(VI) cannot be ascribed to the static quenching process owing to non-new substances forming [31]. Fig. 5(b) shows that the fluorescence lifetime of N,S-BCDs without and with the addition of quench agent are 11.66 ns and 8.663 ns, indicating the decrease of lifetime, which reveals the existence of energy transfer in the quenching process [32]. The literature reports suggest the decrease of the luminescence lifetime on addition of quencher to the fluorophore is ascribed to dynamic quenching mechanism [32]. Based on the above analysis, we can speculate that the collision between luminescence molecules and the quencher just affects the excited state, and the absorption spectrum of the luminescence molecules remain the same, while the excited state lifetime of the fluorescent molecules will decrease in the presence of the quencher [33]. These characteristics are sufficient to prove that the quenching mechanism of Cr(VI) for N,S-BCDs is dynamic quenching. And the quenching mechanism of N,S-BCDs for Cr(VI) is shown in Fig.S2.

Bright blue luminescence N,S-BCDs with an average size of 3.8 nm were synthesized by one-pot green hydrothermal method using grapefruit juice as carbon source and urea as nitrogen dopant. The obtained N,S-BCDs with a high QY of 84.93% indicated excitation-dependent emission behavior and a high concentration quenching effect. More importantly, N,S-BCDs from grapefruit juice possess high sensitivity and selectivity toward the detection of Cr(VI) with LOD determined as 0.155 μM. Furthermore, paper-based sensors constructed by these N,S-BCDs were useful for the detection of 0.98 mM Cr (VI), indicating potential for applications in wastewater and food samples.

Shipeng Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Xinzhu Huo: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Haoran Zhao: Resources, Data curation, Visualization. Yahui Dong: Resources, Data curation, Visualization. Qian Cheng: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Yu Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Grant No.2572019BB06); Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province (Grant No. LH2019E002).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.chphi.2022.100112.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simple electrochemical sensing for mercury ions in dairy product using optimal Cu2+-based metal-organic frameworks as signal reporting
J. Hazard. Mater.

Han, Simple electrochemical sensing for mercury ions in dairy product using optimal Cu2+-based metal-organic frameworks as signal reporting, J. Hazard. Mater. 400 (2020) 123222. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2020.123222.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review on cleaner strategies for chromium industrial wastewater: Present research and future perspective
J. Clean. Prod.

Talanta

Anal. Sci.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly selective colorimetric and electrochemical sensing of iron (III) using Nile red functionalized graphene film
Biosens. Bioelectron.
